title: How I'm Using AI to Auto-Tag, Review, and Plan Every Trade — $26,000+ in Prop Firm Payouts
author: Kasraborhan
selftext:
I've been trading futures for a few years now, mostly NQ and MNQ. Last 30 days I'm up $3,745 across 64 trades with a 47% win rate. Sounds okay on the surface. Then I let an AI loose on my journal data and it basically told me I'm doing the right thing 12% of the time and lighting money on fire the other 88%.

Here's exactly what it found.

# "You're taking far more trades than you're classifying as valid setups"

Between May 5 and June 4, I logged 66 trades. The AI cross-referenced every one of them against my playbook strategies. Only 8 out of 66 were attached to a defined strategy. That's 87.9% of my trades running with no structured setup behind them.

Here's where it gets ugly. Those 8 tagged trades? They made **+$1,315.69.**

* **15MIN ORB:** 6 trades, 66.7% win rate, +$806.51, profit factor 3.6
* **ERL → IRL:** 2 trades, 100% win rate, +$509.18

The AI pulled up specific examples,M a short MNQ that lost $226 in 5 minutes, another short that lost $106 in 5 minutes, a long that lost $235 in 21 seconds. Twenty one seconds. It flagged that one specifically and said "this is not a playbook trade, it's a reactive decision."

The message was clear: my edge lives inside my playbook. 

# "Your longs are your edge. Your shorts are a drag."

The AI broke down my long vs short performance and it wasn't even close.

* **Longs:** 49 trades, 56.82% win rate, avg win $696
* **Shorts:** 15 trades, 20.00% win rate

A 20% short win rate. The AI straight up said "this is not tradable." It pulled the recent damage, two shorts on June 3rd that lost $187 and $191 back to back. Then it showed me my best trades from the same period, all longs that I held patiently: +$327 held 5 hours, +$340 held 3.5 hours, +$371 held 13 hours, +$306 held 43 minutes. The pattern was obvious. When I trade with the trend and hold, I get paid. When I flip short reactively near the open, I bleed.

# The AI auto-tagger 

Every trade that gets imported into my journal now runs through an AI auto-tagger. It reads the trade data and applies tags automatically based on rules I set, things like "revenge trade" if two losses cluster within 15 minutes, "dead zone" for entries between 7-8AM, "green to red" for trades that were profitable before closing as a loss, and "unplanned" for anything without a matching setup.

One entry from this week: a short MNQ entered at 7:06 AM during the London session got tagged with five flags simultaneously, Dead Zone entry, pre-market restriction, Unplanned, Green to Red, and no defined setup structure. That's one trade breaking five rules at once. Without the tagger I probably would've moved on and done it again tomorrow.

Another one caught an MNQ long taken during Asia session with no stop loss and no profit target, held for 3+ hours before closing as a loss. Tagged it as extended hold, pre-market entry, and unplanned. The description it generated was brutally specific about why the trade had no business being taken.

# It built me a game plan for today based on all of this

After reviewing everything, the AI gave me three rules for today's session:

**1. Trade long-only unless the market is overwhelmingly obvious.** My stats don't support discretionary shorts right now. If I see both a long and short case, take the long or pass.

**2. No entry without confirmation.** My recent bad trades failed almost immediately. No clicking into the first move just because price is moving. Wait for level interaction, reclaim, or exact setup trigger. If I can't define my invalidation before entry, skip it.

**3. Prioritize the hold over the scalp.** My better trades lately came from letting winners work. Once in a good long, don't cut it just because it goes a little green. Let the trade prove it's wrong before exiting.

Then it gave me a clean session plan: bias long-first, avoid reactive shorts and revenge entries, focus on patient long confirmation, and main objective, protect the day from a loss cluster.

# What actually impressed me

Most "AI trading tools" I've seen are either glorified chatbots that give generic advice or backtesting wrappers with an AI label slapped on. What surprised me about this one is that it's reading my actual trades, my actual rules, my actual patterns  and then calling me out with specific trade IDs, exact timestamps, and dollar amounts.

comments.author: Labzz45
comments.body: This is definitely cool but most likely fake

replies.author: Kasraborhan
replies.body: Why is it fake? its the new Tradezella AI that does all this!
